cards.grammarNote: Emotional states in Korean are expressed with verbs and adjective verbs. All take the same polite -아요/-어요 ending. 기분이 좋아요 = I feel good (lit: my mood is good).
기분 = mood/feeling. 이 = subject marker. 좋다 → 좋아요. 기분이 나빠요 = I'm in a bad mood (나쁘다 → 나빠요).
울다 (to cry) + -고 싶다 (want to) → 울고 싶어요. 싶다 → 싶어요. 웃고 싶어요 = I want to laugh.
화 = anger. 가 = subject marker. 나다 (to come out/arise) → 나요. 화가 나다 = anger arises = to get angry. Very natural expression.
스트레스 = stress. 받다 (to receive) → 받아요. 스트레스 받아요 = lit: I'm receiving stress = I'm stressed. Very common expression.
행복하다 (to be happy) → 행복해요. 하다 adjective → 해요. 행복 = happiness. 행복하지 않아요 = I'm not happy.
설레다 (to feel excited/fluttery) → 설레요. A positive emotional anticipation — before a date, travel, or good news. Not the same as being nervous about failure (걱정돼요).
걱정 = worry. 되다 (to become) → 돼요. 걱정되다 = to become worried = to be worried. 걱정하지 마요 = don't worry.
우울하다 (to be depressed) → 우울해요. 하다 adjective → 해요. 기분이 우울해요 = I'm feeling depressed.
외롭다 (to be lonely) → 외로워요. ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ → 우. 너무 외로워요 = I'm so lonely.
보다 (to see) + -고 싶다 (want to) → 보고 싶어요 = I want to see you / I miss you. Literally: 'I want to see (you)'. Very commonly used in Korean.
고맙다 (to be thankful) → 고마워요. ㅂ-irregular: ㅂ → 우. A warmer and more personal thank-you than 감사해요.
이제 = now. 기분이 = mood (subject). 낫다 (to get better) → 나아요. ㅅ-irregular: ㅅ drops before vowel endings. 기분이 나아요 = mood has improved.
